message:
refactor(writing): polish article content and integrate tooltip system

Comprehensive improvements across all article components to enhance
readability, interactivity, and educational value.

Article integrations:
- hoeffding-article.tsx: Integrate math tooltips for statistical terms,
  improve section structure and visual hierarchy
- overprompting-article.tsx: Add jargon tooltips for AI/ML concepts,
  enhance constraint visualization captions
- bakery-article.tsx: Fix JSX template literal syntax in code blocks,
  integrate probability term tooltips
- barra-article.tsx: Minor layout adjustments for factor card display
- cmaes-article.tsx: Expand technical content, improve code examples
- raptorq-article.tsx: Refine math notation and explanation flow

Visualization enhancements:
- bakery-visualizations.tsx: Improve animation timing and transitions
- barra-jargon.tsx: Expand factor model terminology coverage
- raptorq-math-tooltip.tsx: Enhance equation explanations

Supporting changes:
- app/writing/cmaes_explainer/page.tsx: Update page metadata
- lib/cmaes-jargon.ts: Add optimization algorithm terminology
- lib/content.ts: Update article descriptions

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

@@ -9,9 +9,11 @@ import { JetBrains_Mono, Bricolage_Grotesque, } from "next/font/google"; +import { RotateCcw, Activity, Cpu, Box } from "lucide-react"; import katex from "katex"; import { CMAESJargon } from "./cmaes-jargon"; import { CMAESMathTooltip } from "./cmaes-math-tooltip"; +import { GranularMath } from "./granular-math"; // Dynamic import visualizations (no SSR) const HeroCMAES = dynamic( @@ -30,6 +32,14 @@ const BenchmarkRunner = dynamic( () => import("./cmaes-visualizations").then((m) => ({ default: m.BenchmarkRunner })), { ssr: false } ); +const NoiseRobustnessViz = dynamic( + () => import("./cmaes-visualizations").then((m) => ({ default: m.NoiseRobustnessViz })), + { ssr: false } +); +const RestartViz = dynamic( + () => import("./cmaes-visualizations").then((m) => ({ default: m.RestartViz })), + { ssr: false } +); // Fonts const crimsonPro = Crimson_Pro({ @@ -207,109 +217,299 @@ export function CMAESArticle() { But there’s a big category of problems where the gradient either doesn’t exist, is meaningless, or is so expensive to approximate that you might as well not bother. In that world—what people call black-box optimization—my favorite tool by a wide margin is CMA-ES, the Covariance Matrix Adaptation Evolution Strategy.

- Roughly speaking, CMA-ES does for nasty, opaque objective functions what gradient descent does for nice smooth ones: it gives you a principled way to turn "I can only evaluate this thing" into "I can reliably walk toward a good solution." + CMA-ES is a Zero-Order optimizer. It doesn’t need to know the slope of the hill; it just needs to know the height at different points. It gives you a principled way to turn "I can only evaluate this thing" into "I can reliably walk toward a good solution." +

+

+ This is an essay about why I like CMA-ES so much, how it works at an intuitive level, where it came from, and why I think it’s still wildly underused in the neural-net world. +

+ + + + + + {/* ========== 1. WHAT IT IS ========== */} +
+ +

+ 1. What CMA-ES Is (And Why You Should Care) +

+

+ At the highest possible level, CMA-ES does this:

+
+

+ It keeps a Gaussian search distribution over your parameter space and iteratively morphs that Gaussian—its mean and its covariance—to concentrate probability mass where the objective looks good. +

+
+

+ Concretely, suppose you have some unknown function that you want to minimize. You tell CMA-ES: +

+
    +
  • the dimension
    • +
  • an initial guess for the parameters (or just "center me in the middle of the search box")
    • +
  • an initial overall step scale
    • +
+

Then, repeatedly:

+
    +
  1. + 1. +
    + Sampling: It samples a small population of candidate points from a multivariate normal: + + + + where is the current mean, is a scalar step-size, and is a full covariance matrix encoding the current "shape" of promising directions. +
    +
    2. +
  2. + 2. +
    + Evaluation: It evaluates your expensive black-box at each point. +
    +
    3. +
  3. + 3. +
    + Ranking: It ranks the samples by how good they were. +
    +
    4. +
  4. + 4. +
    + Adaptation: It nudges , , and so that the next Gaussian puts more probability mass where the good samples came from and less where the bad ones came from. +
    +
    5. +
-
-
-

- 01. The Search Logic -

-

- At the highest possible level, CMA-ES keeps a Gaussian search distribution over your parameter space and iteratively morphs that Gaussian—its mean and its covariance—to concentrate probability mass where the objective looks good. + + +

+ Over time, the initially spherical Gaussian turns into a rotated, stretched ellipsoid that lines up with the valleys and ridges of your objective function. It’s implicitly learning something like the local Hessian without ever seeing a derivative. +

+

+ Early on, samples are spread out, the covariance is mostly spherical, and the algorithm is "groping in the dark." As evidence accumulates about which directions lead to better values, the ellipsoid tilts, stretches, and shrinks, zooming in on promising regions. +

+ +
+ + + + {/* ========== 2. NO GRADIENT ========== */} +
+ +

+ 2. What It Means Not to Have a Gradient +

+

+ When you first learn optimization, "no gradient" sounds like a minor annoyance. Just finite-difference it, right? Wrong. In the real world, the mapping from parameters to outcome is often a jagged, discontinuous landscape where derivatives are meaningless. +

+ +
+
+
+ +
+

Airplane Wings

+

+ Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulations take hours on a cluster. Meshing and turbulence model switching create sharp discontinuities. One gradient step could take days of compute. +

+
+ +
+
+ +
+

Suspension Bridges

+

+ Finite element models with mode-crossing and discrete material choices. Sharp changes when constraints flip from satisfied to violated. Gradient descent is helpless against brittle constraints.

-
    -
  • - 1. - Sample a small population of candidate points. -
    • -
  • - 2. - Evaluate your expensive black-box at each point. -
    • -
  • - 3. - Rank the samples by how good they were. -
    • -
  • - 4. - Nudge the distribution to follow the "best" survivors. -
    • -
+
+ +
+
+
+
+ +
+

Transformer Hyperparameters

+

+ Layer counts, attention heads, and activation choices are inherently discrete. Training runs are ruinously expensive. Your evaluation budget is maybe a couple hundred points total. +

+
+
+
+
200
+
Max Simulations
+
+
-

- Over time, the initially spherical Gaussian turns into a rotated, stretched ellipsoid that lines up with the valleys and ridges of your objective function. It’s implicitly learning something like the local Hessian without ever seeing a derivative. +

+ You absolutely cannot afford to do "naive" grid search either. A modest grid with 10 values per dimension and 10 dimensions is simulations—ludicrous. But you can afford something like a few thousand simulations, if each one moves you in an intelligently chosen direction.

- +
- {/* ========== VISUAL 01 ========== */} + {/* ========== 3. SLOW MOTION WALKTHROUGH ========== */}

- Visualizing the Loop + 3. CMA-ES in Slow Motion: Designing a Wing in 3D

+

+ Let’s go back to the wing, but drastically simplify to keep things concrete. Suppose we compress all our design freedom into just three scalars: +

+
    +
  1. : normalized aspect ratio in
    2. +
  2. : normalized sweep angle in
    3. +
  3. : normalized airfoil family index in
    4. +
+

+ Normalizing everything to roughly comparable ranges is important because CMA-ES’s initial Gaussian is isotropic. If one variable lives on a scale of 0.001 and another on 10, you’re already telling the algorithm that some directions are "long" and some "short" before it has seen any data. I like my ignorance to be symmetric. +

+ +

The Gradient Hierarchy

+

+ Let’s be clear: If you have a clean, cheap gradient, you should almost always use it. First-order methods like Adam or SGD are incredibly efficient because they know exactly which way is downhill. +

- CMA-ES operates in a generational cycle. It's not just moving a point; it's evolving a belief about the landscape. Walk through the four key phases of a single generation below. + CMA-ES is for the world where the gradient is missing, expensive, or fake (numerical noise). The comparison below isn’t about replacing gradients; it’s about showing how CMA-ES provides a robust fallback when local slopes are too deceptive to trust.

- +

- The crucial thing is: CMA-ES only needs function evaluations. It never asks you for gradients, Jacobians, or any structural information about your model or simulator. That makes it an excellent default whenever evaluations are expensive or the landscape is ugly. + By Generation 5, something interesting happens. The covariance is no longer the identity. Its eigenvectors roughly align with directions in which the objective varies gently vs sharply.

+

+ In the wing case, you might find that changing aspect ratio and sweep together along some diagonal barely changes performance, but moving orthogonally is catastrophic. CMA-ES captures that by turning the sampling ellipsoid into a rotated cigar aligned with the benign direction. +

+
+

+ It’s approximating the local curvature of your objective without ever forming a Hessian and without relying on gradients. +

+
- {/* ========== COMPARISON ========== */} + {/* ========== INVARIANCES ========== */}

- The Advantage of Adaptation + The Invariance Superpower +

+

+ One reason CMA-ES is so robust is its Invariance properties. It is designed to be "coordinate-free." +

+
+
+

Translation & Rotation

+

+ If you rotate your entire search space or move your starting point, CMA-ES will find the exact same path. It doesn’t care about the labels on your axes. +

+
+
+

Rank-Based Scaling

+

+ Because it only uses the rank of samples, it is invariant to any strictly increasing transform of the objective. Optimizing is the same as or . +

+
+
+

+ This makes it one of the few "set and forget" optimizers. You don’t need to spend hours tuning the scales of your parameters or preprocessing your objective values. +

+ +
+ + + + {/* ========== NOISE ========== */} +
+ +

+ Filtering the Noise

- Standard gradient descent is like a person walking down a hill by only looking at their feet. If the hill is a long, narrow, twisted valley, they'll bounce off the walls (oscillate). CMA-ES looks at the geometry of the valley itself. + In many engineering problems, your black-box isn’t a perfect math function. It’s a simulation with rounding errors, or a physical experiment with sensors. It is Stochastic—jittery and noisy.

- + + +

+ Gradient-based methods often choke on noise because they rely on a single local estimate. CMA-ES uses its entire population to average out the jitter, seeing through the "fog" to find the underlying global structure. +

+ +
+ + + + {/* ========== MULTIMODALITY ========== */} +
+ +

+ Escaping the Local Trap +

+

+ Most real-world landscapes aren’t simple bowls. They are Multimodal—full of jagged peaks and deceptive Local Minima that can trap a small population. CMA-ES solves this with a "Restart with Increasing Population" strategy (IPOP). +

+ + + +

+ If progress stalls, the algorithm "explodes" its search radius and sends out a much larger army of scouts. This transition from local exploitation to global exploration is what allows CMA-ES to find the deepest valley in a massive mountain range. +

- {/* ========== THREE EXAMPLES ========== */} + {/* ========== 4. ORIGINS ========== */}

- Where Gradients Go to Die + 4. Where CMA-ES Came From

- When you first learn optimization, "no gradient" sounds like a minor annoyance. Just finite-difference it, right? Wrong. In many real-world problems, finite differences are mostly lies. + CMA-ES didn’t appear out of nowhere. It’s the product of several converging lines of thought.

-
-
-

Airplane Wings

-

- CFD simulations take hours. Meshing and turbulence models create discontinuities. Finite differences are drowned in numerical noise. +

+
+

Evolution Strategies

+

+ While classical genetic algorithms focused on bitstrings and crossover, the evolution strategies community in Germany emphasized continuous search spaces and Gaussian mutations. CMA-ES, introduced by Hansen & Ostermeier in the mid-1990s, pushed this to its logical conclusion: adapt a full covariance matrix.

-
-

Suspension Bridges

-

- Finite element models with mode-crossing and discrete material choices. Sharp changes when constraints flip from satisfied to violated. + +

+

Information Geometry

+

+ If you think of CMA-ES as moving a distribution in the manifold of Gaussians, the right answer is the natural gradient: perform steepest ascent with respect to the Fisher information metric. Akimoto et al. showed that the core CMA-ES update is exactly a sampled natural-gradient step.

-
-

Transformers

-

- Hyperparameters like layer counts and attention heads are inherently discrete. Training runs are ruinously expensive. + +

+

Kriging & Gaussian Processes

+

+ CMA-ES shares a "Gaussian + covariance + unbiasedness" mindset with Kriging (GP regression). While Kriging models the function itself, CMA-ES uses the Gaussian as a proposal distribution to sample points with low objective values.

@@ -318,84 +518,159 @@ export function CMAESArticle() { - {/* ========== THE MECHANICS ========== */} + {/* ========== 5. TWO WORLDS ========== */}

- The Mechanics of Adaptation + 5. Two Optimization Worlds That Barely Talk

- For any concrete choice of parameters, we sample candidate points from a distribution: + One of the weird sociological facts about CMA-ES is that it lives, culturally, in a different universe from most deep-learning folks.

- - - -

- Where is the current mean, is a scalar step-size, and is a full covariance matrix encoding the current "shape" of promising directions. +

+
+

+ GECCO World +

+

+ The evolutionary computation community. They talk about EDAs, surrogate models, and CMA-ES as the primary language of optimization. +

+
+
+

+ NeurIPS World +

+

+ Deep learning practitioners. They know backprop and SGD. They know Bayesian optimization, but evolutionary computation feels like a different discipline. +

+
+
+

+ This siloing is mostly historical accident. There’s an interesting overlap between how CMA-ES thinks about learning a distribution and how LeCun’s Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPA) think about predicting missing parts of an image.

+ +
-
-

Core Philosophy

-

- "Encode everything as continuous real numbers in a normalized box, and push all the weirdness into your encode/decode procedure." -

-
+ + {/* ========== 6. DEEP LEARNING HYBRIDS ========== */} +
+ +

+ 6. CMA-ES + Deep Learning + Creative Systems +

- CMA-ES doesn’t care if you’re optimizing integers, categories, or continuous bounds. As long as you can map them into a unit hypercube, the algorithm treats it as a smooth-ish landscape of likelihood. + The irony is that CMA-ES is an incredibly natural companion to modern DL systems once you stop insisting that everything be end-to-end differentiable.

+ +

Tuning Continuous Cellular Automata

+

+ Imagine designing a continuous CA with interesting visual dynamics. You want to score patterns along dimensions like complexity, persistence, or "aesthetic interest." +

+

+ There are no gradients that connect these high-level properties to underlying parameters. But you can map the parameters into a unit hypercube and let CMA-ES search. It doesn’t understand "visual interest"; it just knows which vectors yield higher scores and deforms its Gaussian to find more of them. +

+ +
+

The Automated Experimental Collaborator

+

+ You write the simulator and the scoring function; CMA-ES does the boring part of exploring the parameter space. It ends up with settings you’d never have guessed by hand-tuning. +

+
- {/* ========== LIVE SOLVER ========== */} + {/* ========== LIVE BENCHMARKS ========== */}

- Live Benchmarks + 7. Live Benchmarks & Implementations

- Watch the algorithm in action across different benchmark landscapes. From the perfectly smooth Sphere to the deceptive Rastrigin, notice how the covariance matrix learns to navigate valleys and avoid local traps. + Watch the algorithm crawl its way through different landscapes while adapting its own notion of "where the promising valleys live."

-
- // Evolution Path Momentum
- p\u03c3 = (1-cs) * p\u03c3 + sqrt(cs*(2-cs)*mueff) * C^{-1/2} * (m_new - m_old) / \u03c3 +

Two Implementations I Built

+
+
+

wasm_cmaes

+

Rust CMA-ES compiled to WebAssembly, wrapped in a clean JS/TS API. Features SIMD-accelerated vector ops and Rayon parallelism inside the WASM module.

+ GitHub Repo +
+
+

fast_cmaes

+

Hyper-optimized Rust core with a first-class Python experience. SIMD, Rayon, deterministic seeds, and a Rich-powered TUI for terminal monitoring.

+ GitHub Repo +
- {/* ========== WHY IT FEELS OPTIMAL ========== */} + {/* ========== TECHNICAL ADDENDUM ========== */}

- Why It Feels "Optimal" + Technical Addendum

-

- CMA-ES isn’t just a clever heuristic; it’s performing the canonical invariant gradient update in distribution space. Akimoto et al. showed that the core CMA-ES update is exactly a sampled natural-gradient step on the manifold of multivariate normals. +

+ Based on Nikolaus Hansen’s 2024 tutorial.

-
-

Information Geometry

-

- If you want to minimize your worst-case regret under near-total ignorance about the objective’s geometry, starting with an isotropic Gaussian and nudging it via natural gradients is about as clean and assumption-free as it gets. -

-
+
+
+

The Search Distribution as Central Object

+

+ You’re not optimizing directly; you’re optimizing a probability distribution over . All adaptation happens in distribution space . +

+
+ +
+

Invariance as Design Principle

+

+ CMA-ES is designed for rank-based invariance: it only uses the ordering of fitness values, making it invariant to any strictly increasing transform of . It is also invariant under rigid linear transforms of the search space. +

+
-

- It’s also closely related to the cross-entropy method for optimization, where you iteratively fit a parametric distribution to elite samples by minimizing KL divergence. -

+
+

Step-size Control (CSA)

+

+ The Cumulative Step-size Adaptation compares the evolution path length to what you’d expect from a random walk. If the path is "too long," it means we’re consistently moving in a correlated direction → increase . +

+
+ +
+ +
+

- - Statistical Topology / Dependency Engine -
-

- My Favorite Statistical Measure: -
- - Hoeffding's D. +
+ +
+ RESEARCH NOTE / 2026.02.12 + + + + The Latent
+ + Geometry -

-

- Standard correlation misses non-linear dependencies. - Hoeffding's D captures the latent geometry where others are blind. -

+
of Data. + + + + Why Hoeffding's is my favorite measure for quantifying the deep dependencies that standard correlation misses. +
-
- - Scroll to Enter the Lab - -
-
+ Initialization +
+ +
- {/* ========== INTRO ========== */} -
+ {/* ========== NARRATIVE START ========== */} +
-

+

Suppose you have two sequences of numbers. You want to know if they are related. In most settings, we have no clue a priori what the nature of that relationship might be. Are they linear? Cyclical? Sinusoidal?

Standard correlation measures—Pearson and Spearman—make massive assumptions. They look for straight lines or monotonic trends. If your data forms a ring or an "X" shape, these measures will often report zero correlation, even though the dependency is obvious to any human eye.

-
-
-
- +
+
+ +
+

The Delta Insight

+

+ The "brilliant" logic behind Hoeffding's is comparing the Joint Distribution of your data to the Product of Marginals. If and are independent, the product of their individual behaviors should perfectly predict their joint behavior. Any deviation from this is dependency. +

-

- The Dependency Engine -

-

- The "brilliant" insight behind Hoeffding's D is comparing the Joint Distribution of your data to the Product of Marginals. If $X$ and $Y$ are independent, the product of their individual behaviors should perfectly predict their joint behavior. Any deviation from this "independent prediction" is dependency. -

- +
{/* ========== THE LAB ========== */}
- -

- Visualizing the Residuals -

-

- Traditional measures are blind to non-monotonic shapes. Below, notice how Pearson fails on the Ring or the X-Shape, while Hoeffding's D identifies the structure by summing the "residual energy" in the heatmap. -

- + +
+
Interactivity 01
+

Visualizing the Residuals

+

+ Switch between topologies to see how standard correlation () fails while Hoeffding's identifies the latent structure. +

+
+ - -

- The heatmap on the right represents the Residuals ($P(X,Y) - P(X)P(Y)$). Intense color pockets indicate that certain combinations of $X$ and $Y$ happen far more (or less) frequently than random chance would allow. Hoeffding's D effectively quantifies the volume of these deviations. -

+ +
+

+ The heatmap on the right represents the Residuals (). Intense color pockets indicate that certain combinations of and happen far more (or less) frequently than random chance would allow. +

+

+ Hoeffding's effectively quantifies the "energy" or volume of these deviations. Notice that even for the Ring, where the average slope is zero, the residual energy is massive. +

+
- +
{/* ========== THE OUTLIER CRUSHER ========== */}
- -

- Taming the Outliers -

-

- Outliers are the nemesis of Pearson correlation. A single point with an extreme value can dominate the variance, making perfectly related points look uncorrelated. Hoeffding's D solves this by operating in Rank Space. -

+ +
+
Robustness Module
+

Taming the Outliers

+

+ Outliers are the nemesis of variance-based measures. Watch how Ranking transforms destructive magnitudes into manageable ordinal data. +

+
-

- By applying ranking, we strip away the destructive magnitude of outliers. A value of 1,000,000 becomes just "Position 5" if there are only 5 points. This transformation is what makes Hoeffding's D so robust in real-world data pipelines. -

- - +
+ +
- +
- {/* ========== THE MATH ========== */} -
+ {/* ========== THE MATHEMATICAL ENGINE ========== */} +
+
-

- The Statistical Engine -

-

- Wassily Hoeffding's 1948 paper defines $D$ through a series of intermediate terms that evaluate every possible quadruple of points in your dataset. -

+
+
The Formalism
+

The Statistical Engine

+

+ Wassily Hoeffding's 1948 paper defines through a series of intermediate terms that evaluate every possible quadruple of points in your dataset. +

+
-
-

- The Normalization Logic -

- -
-
-
-

- Universal Consistency + Universal Logic
-

As $N$ grows, $D$ is guaranteed to identify any non-independent distribution, regardless of its shape.

+

+ As grows, is mathematically guaranteed to identify any non-independent distribution, regardless of its topological complexity. +

-

Evaluating quadruples (n choose 4) is expensive. For N=5,000, we evaluate ~6.2 billion combinations.

- Live Executing Logic -

-

- The implementation can be surprisingly concise. This live TypeScript console computes Hoeffding's D for the height/weight dataset mentioned earlier. -

+ +
+
Implementation
+

The TypeScript Kernel

+

+ The magic of Hoeffding's is that such high-level topological analysis can be expressed in surprisingly few lines of code. +

+
- -

- Pedagogically, this demonstrates that statistical complexity doesn't always mean complex code. The "magic" is in the ranking and the triple-summation. For large-scale production (millions of vectors), I've written a high-performance Rust implementation available on my GitHub. -

- +
- {/* ========== SEMANTIC SIMILARITY ========== */} -
+ {/* ========== AI SECTION ========== */} +
+
-

- A Better Metric for AI -

-

- In the world of LLMs and RAG, Cosine Similarity is the workhorse. It is excellent for filtering millions of vectors down to a manageable "needle in a haystack." -

-

- But geometric intuition fails in high dimensions. In 2048-dimensional embedding space, nearly all of a sphere's volume is concentrated near its surface. This makes cosine similarity a blunt instrument for the final ranking. -

-
- Hoeffding's D allows us to rank the final candidates based on deep, non-linear dependency rather than just geometric alignment. +

A Better Metric for AI

+ +
+

+ In the world of LLMs and RAG, Cosine Similarity is the workhorse. It is excellent for filtering millions of vectors down to a manageable "needle in a haystack." +

+ +

+ "But geometric intuition fails in high dimensions. In 2048-dimensional embedding space, nearly all of a sphere's volume is concentrated near its surface. This makes cosine similarity a blunt instrument for final ranking." +

+ +
+ Hoeffding's allows us to rank the final candidates based on deep, non-linear dependency rather than just geometric alignment. It is the gold standard for quantifying true association in the modern data age. +
-

- It is symmetric, bounded between -0.5 and 1.0, and universal. It is the gold standard for quantifying dependency in the modern data age. -

- {/* Footer spacing */} -
+
+ +
Conclusion
+

+ Universal, robust, and topologically aware. Hoeffding's remains the most elegant solution to the problem of "knowing if things are related." +

+    long: "The property where an algorithm's behavior remains identical even if the problem is rotated, scaled, or its values are transformed by a monotonic function.",
+    why: "It means the algorithm doesn't require the user to perfectly scale or orient their parameters before starting.",

+    long: "A state where some parameters are much more sensitive than others (e.g., changing x by 0.01 matters as much as changing y by 100).",
+    why: "Most optimizers fail here; CMA-ES handles it by stretching its search ellipsoid to match these differing scales.",
+    analogy: "Trying to find the bottom of a canyon that is 10 miles long but only 2 feet wide."

+    why: "It allows the algorithm to transition from a local search to a global search, eventually finding the global minimum of complex, multimodal functions.",
+    analogy: "If a small group of scouts gets trapped in a local valley, send out a much larger army to search the entire mountain range."
+  },

+    short: "Handling 'noisy' feedback",

+    why: "Real-world simulations are often noisy; CMA-ES's population-based averaging naturally filters out this noise.",
+    analogy: "Finding the highest point on a sand dune while a windstorm is constantly shifting the grains."
+  },

+    long: "Algorithms that only require the output f(x) for a given input x, with no knowledge of the function's internal derivative.",
+    why: "Essential for black-box systems like simulators where you cannot 'see inside' to compute a gradient.",
+    analogy: "Tuning a guitar by ear rather than using a tuner that tells you exactly how many Hz you are off."
+  },
+  "first-order": {
+    term: "First-Order Optimization",
+    short: "Using gradient information",
+    long: "Algorithms like Gradient Descent that use the first derivative (the gradient) to move directly toward the minimum.",
+    why: "If a meaningful gradient exists, first-order methods are usually orders of magnitude faster and more precise.",
+    analogy: "Having a GPS that tells you the exact direction to the destination, vs. walking around and seeing if you're getting warmer or colder."

+    short: "Sampled From",

+    analogy: "The 'shape' of the cloud of scouts."
+  },
+  "mean-vector": {

+    short: "Distribution Mean",

+    analogy: "The base camp for the scouts."
+  },
+  "sigma-sq": {
+    term: "\u03c3\u00b2",
+    short: "Variance Scale",
+    long: "The square of the step-size, providing the global scaling factor for the search.",
+    analogy: "The overall 'reach' or 'zoom level' of the exploration."
+  },
+  "evolution-path": {

+    why: "It tracks momentum and correlation between steps to decide when to increase or decrease the global step-size.",
+    analogy: "The smoothed trail left by the base camp as it moves."
+  },
+  "learning-rate-cs": {

+    short: "Step-size Learning Rate",
+    long: "Controls how quickly the evolution path (and thus the step-size) adapts to new information.",
+    analogy: "How much weight we give to the latest scouts' discovery vs. historical knowledge."
+  },
+  "selection-mass": {
+    term: "\u03bc_eff",
+    short: "Selection Mass",
+    long: "The effective number of samples used to update the distribution, accounting for the weights assigned to each.",
+    analogy: "The strength of the 'voting block' among the elite scouts."
+  },
+  "whitening": {
+    term: "C^{-1/2}",
+    short: "Whitening Transform",
+    long: "The inverse square root of the covariance matrix, used to normalize the step into a spherical coordinate system.",
+    why: "This allows the algorithm to measure 'distance' in a way that is independent of the current search ellipsoid's orientation.",
+    analogy: "Squashing the cigar-shaped cloud back into a sphere so we can see the 'true' direction of progress."
